tweets.text:
March 2020 OpEx was Friday, March 20 — quarterly quad witching, mid-crash. The setup into it: SPX -15% that week (worst since 2008), and OpEx day itself fell -4.3% with a hard sell into the close as record put open interest rolled off. Closed 2304.92.

Next day — Monday, March 23: the bottom of the entire COVID bear market.

•ES went limit-down again Sunday night; the Fed announced unlimited QE pre-market Monday; the cash market still flushed to 2191.86 intraday — the low of the crash — and closed -2.9% at 2237.40.

Next two weeks:

•Mar 24: +9.4%, best day since Oct 2008 (Dow +11.4%, best since 1933)
•Mar 26: +6.2% — the three-day rally off the low exceeded +20% intraday, technically the fastest “new bull market” ever
•Week 1 post-OpEx (Mar 23–27): +10.3%, best week since 2009
•Week 2 was digestion: Apr 1 fell -4.4%, and two weeks out (Apr 3) SPX sat at 2488.65 — +8.0% from the OpEx close, +13.5% from the Monday low. It never looked back.

So for the thread’s purposes: the single most important market turn of the decade occurred on the first trading day after quarterly OpEx. The structural read is clean — enormous put OI expired Friday, dealer short-gamma hedges unwound, charm/vanna tailwinds kicked in, and record quarter-end pension rebalancing buying hit the following week. The market was “unpinned.”

The confound is equally enormous, though: unlimited QE landed the morning of the 23rd and the $2.2T CARES Act was signed the 27th. You can’t cleanly separate gamma mechanics from the largest policy bazooka in history firing the same week. What keeps the pattern interesting is the rhyme: December 2018 also bottomed the Monday after quad witching (Dec 24, then +5% on Dec 26), and June 2022’s local low printed on OpEx Friday itself. Post-quarterly-OpEx windows recurrently host violent turns — March 2020 is just the most extreme specimen.

Sept just March?

tweets.text:
TVL is the laziest way to evaluate a protocol.

That becomes clear when you compare @LidoFinance and @jito_sol.

Jito ($786M) vs Lido ($17.7B).
Lido is 22x larger by TVL.

But their annual fees?

→ Lido: $740M
→ Jito: $259M

Lido mostly earns fees from staking rewards on deposited ETH.

Jito earns fees from staking + MEV + priority fees through its block engine on Solana.

Most of Jito's fees come from MEV and priority fees across Solana's blockspace, so it earns more per dollar deposited.

Capital size ≠ capital efficiency.

tweets.text:
Just dropped a practical breakdown of founder growth on X.

Here’s your TL;DR ↓

- In B2B, buying decisions are shaped by hidden buyers, internal champions, and execs – not just the visible contact.

- For crypto teams, X still matters. X, Telegram, and YouTube make up 84% of crypto community social time.

- In fact, founder growth is a trust system, not a posting-frequency problem.

- The common failure mode is volume without position – daily posts, recycled threads, and AI-polished founder copy.

- At @GREEND0TS we usually map the fix into seven layers: expertise zones, expertise circles, tone of voice, long-form authority, QTs/comments, relationship density, and dynamic optimization.

- AI is useful for research, clustering, and first drafts. It should not replace the founder’s judgment, editing, or point of view.

- The real signal is relationship density: replies, DMs, intros, and inbound from the right circles matter more than raw follower count.

tweets.text:
Feels bad, -49.4% drawdown this month after the recent crash.

My portfolio is mainly AI chokepoints and bottlenecks. 

In the memory, photonics, robotics, and upstream semis, (on margin) which all tend to be higher beta than others. But reduced leverage recently from the crash.

I see a lot of people making fun of the drop or AI names, saying it’s obvious that:

- “AI is a bubble”
- “memory/kospi is a bubble”
- “photonics is a bubble”
- “humanoids won’t get anywhere”
- “neoclouds will get replaced by hyperscalers like Meta”

And a bunch of retail + bots saying “sell everything, it’s never going to recover”.

But I have conviction that all these themes are backed by structural revenue growth or technological shifts.

And I’ve had similar drawdowns back when there admin threatened global tariffs, before markets pulled off a recovery.

I personally have a longer horizon + higher tolerance for volatility than others, to see how this plays out.

Especially considering a lot of retail view things on a week to week basis: no, my thesis isn’t wrong yet if I project revenue inflection in H2 2027 and it’s 2026 now. 

Anyway, feels bad short term just wanted to share anyway for transparency.

Agreed! It’s nice to remember your thesis during a market crash.

From my own personal thesis, if $AAOI hits $1.4B quarterly revenue start of Q3 2027. Which is annualized $5.6B off a $8B MC. 

Is it “over” for the company if that revenue ramp hasn’t even shown up in the quarterly earnings… when it’s 2026?

Same applies to my CPO sector exposure like $SIVE, an architecture shift led by $NVDA. 

If scale out volume ramps from H2 2026 into 2027, and scale up heavily volume ramps into 2028.

Is it “over” that $0 -> $91B TAM expansion (per GS) hasn’t even hit yet?

With robotics like Agility, is it over in 2026 if the listing hasn’t even happened yet and humanoids haven’t ramped?

I personally think current market conditions are a reflection of liquidity and leverage, not individual fundamentals.

It’s brutal for everyone to see KOSPI, TW, Nikkei, and AI, space, robotics sector stocks crash recently. 

Especially when there’s a lot of irrational behavior stemming from those leverage.

In the end, we can’t tell you what stocks to buy, what timeframe you should sell, how to size your positions, or what you should do. 

Only share personal thoughts or research and track if they get validated over time.

So it’s extremely important to build your own thesis, since everyone has unique risk tolerance or investing timeframes.

And that usually leads to having higher conviction during crashes.

tweets.text:
Engineers at Coinbase are getting closer to recursive self-improvement, or loops.

For instance, agents can now ingest and summarize customer feedback collected right in the app, prioritize bugs and features based on that feedback, draft the code, security review it, and provide it to a human engineer for final review. All happens automatically each day and it learns from the human edits to improve over time.

We're finding more areas to introduce loops across the company. Instead of prompting agents with what to do, you can increasingly give them a goal, and they bring back high quality work for you to review.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
The fundamentals of engineering haven't changed, but the daily work has.

We're managing agents, building loops and taste and judgment matter more than ever. 

So we rebuilt how we hire engineers from scratch to match how we build. Here's what we've been up to @ Coinbase:

tweets.text:
Great discussion with the top execs from crypto’s top trade associations at @hlglobal_ yesterday.

We covered the Clarity Act; everyone wants the bill done, and a handful of issues remain outstanding with time running short. My fellow panelists were still optimistic about passage before August recess…more optimistic than me.

We also talked SEC/CFTC; both agencies are well informed and working hard on Project Crypto. No matter which way Clarity goes, they will each have a lot to do in the coming months and years.

Thank you @lindsayfraser0 @_jikim @CodyCarboneDC for your hard work and taking the time during a busy week! 🙏

tweets.text:
2026 is the year enterprises require multi-model support in every AI purchasing decision.

Betting on one provider is too risky when the frontier keeps changing hands. Sometimes OpenAI is ahead, sometimes Anthropic, and for many smaller tasks an open-source model is already good enough. Enterprises do not need Einstein-level intelligence to classify an email, summarize a document, or update a spreadsheet.

At the frontier, a model can command a 90% premium. But once it is no longer at the frontier, it loses its pricing power and has to compete on cost, pushing inference toward thinner margins.

That is why the defining enterprise AI infrastructure of 2026 will be the harness: model-agnostic systems that evaluate each task and route it to the best model for intelligence, speed, security, and cost. Multi-model will become a baseline procurement requirement.

Also if intelligence really becomes a commodity that is too cheap to meter, it could generate enormous revenue while still looking economically more like a utility: massive consumption, massive infrastructure, but thin margins.

The challenge is that today’s high inference margins at the frontier help fund the enormous compute required to train the next generation of models. Compress those margins too quickly, and labs have less capital to buy compute. Less compute could mean slower progress at the frontier, creating a vicious cycle.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Kimi K3 may be an important inflection point for AI. Potentially negative for Anthropic and OpenAI while being net positive for essentially every other company in the world. I mean that very literally. Although the real “Sputnik moment” would be an open-source frontier model that https://t.co/tdAkU4oRgo

tweets.text:
This is where the rubber hits the road in Coastal Metropolitan areas.  

The infrastructure was built 100 yrs ago and most of these cities are so expensive now, fixing the infrastructure doesn't actually make fiscal sense when you add in the cost vs. all the other budgeted items.

This is part of the generational arbitrage.

Texas has a massive, record-setting "rainy day" fund balance of over $24 billion, in addition to a recent multi-billion dollar budget surplus used for historic property tax relief. In contrast, California faced persistent structural deficits that required a revised $351.7 billion state budget to achieve a balanced status with zero deficit through mid-2028.

Living in NYC, Chicago, LA or SF is like a tightening vice on anyone that owns property there.

It's a slow motion generational trainwreck especially for public employees like cops, firefighters & teachers!!
